characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.description: The Alcoholic: He relies on alcohol far more than someone in such a prominent position really should, resulting in him suffering more than one embarrassing episode of public drunkenness during the run of the series. Not helping is that once he gets drunk, he getsdrunk. And then there was that time he couldn't go without on a visit to a Muslim state, where alcohol isforbidden... Character Development: In Season 1 ofYes, Ministerhe's idealistic to the point of naivety, only managing to get one unequivocal victory over Sir Humphrey in "Big Brother", thanks to aCrazy Enough to Workgambit on live television. Season 2 has him still a little on the idealistic side, but displaying more an interest in what he can personally get out of the job, including an honorary doctorate from Sir Humphrey's former collegenote(Humphrey admittedly engineered this as part of a larger scheme to save the college from closure, but Hacker nonetheless seizes the opportunity with both hands). By Season 3 he's become a lot more politically savvy, and it eventually culminates in his blowing a comparatively minor European directive over the naming of UK sausages out of proportion into a national scandal, with his solving it cementing himself the Prime Minister's job. He also becomes more capable of understanding Sir Humphrey'sSesquipedalian Loquaciousnessspeeches as the series goes on, having become pretty good at interpreting Humphrey's speaking style by the time ofYes, Prime Minister. Dirty Coward: He often has shades of this; in many cases, the problem is something he could fight for, but he's afraid of losing votes if he does so. It's quite common among politicians in the series, to the point where something being described as 'courageous' is the most terrifying thing a politician can hear, even worse than "controversial".Sir Humphrey:"Controversial" only means "this will lose you votes"; "courageous" means "this will lose you the election". Hidden Depths: For all his flaws, Hackerdidwin an elec

characters.name: Hacker
characters.description: The Alcoholic: He relies on alcohol far more than someone in such a prominent position really should, resulting in him suffering more than one embarrassing episode of public drunkenness during the run of the series. Not helping is that once he gets drunk, he getsdrunk. And then there was that time he couldn't go without on a visit to a Muslim state, where alcohol isforbidden... Character Development: In Season 1 ofYes, Ministerhe's idealistic to the point of naivety, only managing to get one unequivocal victory over Sir Humphrey in "Big Brother", thanks to aCrazy Enough to Workgambit on live television. Season 2 has him still a little on the idealistic side, but displaying more an interest in what he can personally get out of the job, including an honorary doctorate from Sir Humphrey's former collegenote(Humphrey admittedly engineered this as part of a larger scheme to save the college from closure, but Hacker nonetheless seizes the opportunity with both hands). By Season 3 he's become a lot more politically savvy, and it eventually culminates in his blowing a comparatively minor European directive over the naming of UK sausages out of proportion into a national scandal, with his solving it cementing himself the Prime Minister's job. He also becomes more capable of understanding Sir Humphrey'sSesquipedalian Loquaciousnessspeeches as the series goes on, having become pretty good at interpreting Humphrey's speaking style by the time ofYes, Prime Minister. Dirty Coward: He often has shades of this; in many cases, the problem is something he could fight for, but he's afraid of losing votes if he does so. It's quite common among politicians in the series, to the point where something being described as 'courageous' is the most terrifying thing a politician can hear, even worse than "controversial".Sir Humphrey:"Controversial" only means "this will lose you votes"; "courageous" means "this will lose you the election". Hidden Depths: For all his flaws, Hackerdidwin an elec

characters.name: Sir Humphrey
characters.description: The Permanent Secretary of the Department of Administrative Affairs, whose goal is to ensure that the Civil Service's agenda is advanced, and the government's agenda goes nowhere. He later gets appointed as the Cabinet Secretary upon the retirement of Sir Arnold Robinson, and goes on to do much the same job there. Appeared in every episode of the show. Almighty Janitor: Despite being anonymous to the population at large and describing himself as a "humble functionary", he is effectively running the country from behind the scenes by the end of the series.President of Buranda:I've always thought that Permanent Under-Secretary is such a demeaning title... makes you sound like an assistant typist or something, whereas you're really in charge of everything, aren't you? Anything but That!: The suggestion of "gardening leave" makes him look like he's been threatened with physical violence. When Dorothy suggests he could voluntarily surrender his honours and just be "MisterAppleby" he flinches. Badass Bureaucrat: He epitomizes this trope. He basically runs the Department of Administrative Affairs and has a huge influence on the British Government, whether working for his Minister or out-gambiting him as an adversary. He eventually rises to Cabinet Secretary where he is the bulk of the power behind the Hacker regime, including getting Hacker promoted to PM in the first place. All while being a "humble functionary". Sir Arnold, prior to retirement, also fits this. Baddie Flattery: He usually praises Hacker's occurrences with a "very droll" remark, right before proceeding to dismiss them.

characters.name: Bernard
characters.description: Beleaguered Bureaucrat: He becomes this frequently, either because of his troubles in balancing out the wishes of his two superiors or having to deal with the more mundane but equally tedious elements of aVast Bureaucracy. Blunt Metaphors Trauma: Often inflicts it, feeling the need to correct Hacker or Sir Humphrey on some metaphor they've made, often earning him aDeath Glarein response. Character Development: InYes Ministerhe's generally rather unworldly and naïve, and is prone to coming out with remarks that make Humphrey doubt whether or not he's really a "high-flyer". ByYes Prime Ministerhe's more experienced and more crafty. Of course, thisisBernard we're talking about, so it's relative, but Humphrey no longer doubts his ability to his face—perhaps because he's more often at odds with Bernard, and can't afford to alienate him. Conflicting Loyalty: He's often torn between obedience to Hacker, who is his immediate superior, and Sir Humphrey, who is his master, and might as well be as unto God to him.Hacker:Let me make one thing clear: Sir Humphrey isnotGod, okay?Bernard:[pause] Will you tell him, or shall I? Could Say It, But...: Often discusses matters in hypothetical or indirect terms when leaking the Minister's plans to Sir Humphrey as a way of getting around the fact that he's supposed to be keeping such information confidential. Only for Humphrey to firmly state "Thank you, Bernard" the moment he's leaked enough details for Humphrey to figure out what Hacker is up to.

characters.name: Annie
characters.description: Deadpan Snarker: She frequently undercuts his pomposity and pretensions with well-timed sarcasm. The Smurfette Principle: She was the only female recurring character fromYes Minister, and one of two female recurring characters (the other being Dorothy Wainwright) fromYes Prime Minister. The Watson: On occasions where Bernard isn't filling the role, she'll serve as someone for Jim to explain the latest problem of the week to. Women Are Wiser: Played with; she's a lot more down to Earth and has a much clearer moral compass than her husband, but she lacks his political nous, meaning that a lot of things that she suggests end up not being practical to implement in reality.

characters.name: Frank
characters.description: Ironic Name: While his surname might imply that he's weaselly and untrustworthy, he actually turns out to have quite a strong sense of morality, ultimately culminating in his resigning and threatening to ruin Hacker's career by blowing open a scandal that the minister and Sir Humphrey agreed to cover up, forcing them tokick him upstairsby persuading him to take charge of a Quango dedicated to reforming the system of governmental organizations. Malicious Misnaming: He's frequently on the receiving end of this from Sir Humphrey (and, to a lesser extent, Annie and Bernard), being referred to as "Mr. Weasel" despite his surname actually being pronounced "Why-sal". Put on a Bus: Due to the writers finding that most of the things he did in the plot could be reassigned to Bernard or Annie without any real difficulty, Frank was written out at the end of the first season by being put in charge of a Quango. Reassigned to Antarctica:Sir Humphrey tries to do this to him in the pilot episode, by putting him in an office block in Walthamstow, eight miles away from the ministry. Hacker doesn't let it fly, though.When it looks like he's going to make a stink about how Sir Humphrey and Hacker are going to save a government industry partner by bribing a banker with a Quango directorship to bail said partner out, they get rid of him by transferring him to run a Quango to investigate Quangos, after which he is never seen again. Sir Humphrey tries to do this to him in the pilot episode, by putting him in an office block in Walthamstow, eight miles away from the ministry. Hacker doesn't let it fly, though.

characters.name: Dorothy
characters.description: The Dreaded: To just about every senior civil servant out there, as she has a habit of effortlessly dismantling anything they tell the Prime Minister. Good Counterpart: To Sir Humphrey inYes, Prime Minister. Her intervention mostly serves to change the power balance of the show by having Hacker more readily willing to rein in Sir Humphrey. Hyper-Competent Sidekick: She's probably the most intelligent and competent person in the entire series. Not one character ever directly gets the better of her, she utterly tears apart any civil servant who tries to pull the wool over her eyes, and her ideas for civil service reform are both sensible and perfectly workable, with Humphrey only managing to stop them by going behind her back, recommending an alternate plan to Hacker, and duping him into not telling Dorothy about it. Reassigned to Antarctica: In her first episode, it turns out that the reason she hadn't shown up in the previous episodes of the season is because Humphrey had successfully pulled on her what he had tried to do to Weisel in the series pilot, moving her office to an out of the way nook where she wouldn't have access to the PM. At the end of the episode Humphrey is forced to give her back her old office in exchange for the restrictions on access to the PM he accidentally placed on himself being lifted as well. Remember the New Guy?: A mild case; dialogue implies that she was also political advisor to the previous PM and was inherited by Hacker when he took over, and that she was feared by senior civil servants in every department. Given that Hacker had his hands full dealing with Sir Humphrey and his own former department, however, it's understandable that he wouldn't know all that much about her.

characters.name: Claire
characters.description: Hyper-Competent Sidekick: Like Dorothy before her, Claire is an astute and capable advisor who is able to help Hacker foil Humphrey's schemes.

characters.name: Sophie
characters.description: Everything Is Racist: She admits that she spent most of her college career getting high and wasting time in greasy spoons, but when Humphrey expresses mild shock that she still graduated with honors, she thinks it's because he's being racist and assuming a black woman couldn't do as well in school as she did. Insistent Terminology: She is not a carer; she is acare worker. Twofer Token Minority: She's female, black, and gay.

characters.name: Martin
characters.description: The Rival: Serves as this to the Prime Minister, who is said to bear a grudge against both Martin and Hacker for Martin's abortive leadership run. Though considering he's Foreign Secretary, the Prime Minister's ire seems to fall mostly on Hacker, who was his campaign manager. What Happened to the Mouse?: He appears twice in the first season of the show and then never appears again, though he's occasionally referenced. By the finale he's been replaced by Duncan at the Foreign Office.

characters.name: Jeffrey
characters.description: Number Two for Brains: While his first appearance has him participate in elevating Hacker to the position of Prime Minister, once Hacker has the job and Jeffrey has to keep the party in line for him, he (Jeffrey) is never shown being useful again. He falls for Humphrey's machinations and lends credence to the false rumors that the Employment Secretary is maneuvering against Hacker, and he also tells Hacker the party wants education reforms but has no useful advice which could actually help with that. Opinion Flip-Flop: He doesn't think anyone in the party is trying to launch a leadership challenge against Hacker, but once Hacker says he thinks his Employment Secretary is doing so, Jeffrey immediately hedges and says it's possible the secretary is doing so after all. Sucksessor: He replaces Vic Gould as the party's Chief Whip, but while Vic was able to keep the party in line and successfully intimidated Hacker into backing down on multiple occasions, Jeffrey is hapless and we never see him actually getting anyone to do what Hacker wants.

characters.name: Bill
characters.description: Unwitting Instigator of Doom: By virtue of being in charge of scheduling Hacker's press events, he often inadvertently contributes to Hacker's problems when the events either don't go as advertised or result in Hacker getting put on the spot and committing to something that will later turn out to be problematic. One noted example is the episode 'The Patron of the Arts,' where he arranges for Hacker to present at an arts gala only for the toastmaster to threaten to insult Hacker in his speech because Hacker's government didn't give the National Theatre more money.

characters.name: Malcolm
characters.description: Hyper-Competent Sidekick: Unlike Bill, Malcolm seems relatively competent and works to properly prepare Hacker for his broadcasts and press events. What Happened to the Mouse?: He's brought in for the first couple episodes ofYes, Prime Ministerbut then abruptly vanishes without explanation and Bill again is shown to be Hacker's press secretary.

characters.name: George
characters.description: Almighty Janitor: Played with. George seems to know everything that Humphrey wishes to keep secret (as such information is apparently common knowledge to Whitehall drivers), but he can't actually do much with that information besides pass it on to Hacker.

characters.name: Dr. Cartwright
characters.description: Hyper-Competent Sidekick: Cartwright is intelligent and knows all the necessary details about what the department should do to reform local government. His willingness to divulge this information to Hacker makes him a threat to Humphrey, who is implied to ultimately get rid of him. Reassigned to Antarctica: After his second appearance it's heavily implied Humphrey arranged for him to be sent far away so that Hacker couldn't consult him anymore. Smart People Wear Glasses: He's one of the most intelligent people Hacker interacts with, he has a doctoral degree, and he's also one of the only characters in the class to always be seen wearing glasses. (Hacker has a pair of reading spectacles but he doesn't otherwise wear glasses).

characters.name: Sir Arnold
characters.description: Age Lift: Sir Arnold is implied to be a good deal older and more experienced than Humphrey, but his actor John Nettleton is actually only a couple of months older than Nigel Hawthorne. This makes for cognitive dissonance when Nettleton shows up in making-of documentaries decades later, looking barely older than he did when he was in the show. Almighty Janitor: InYes, Prime Ministerhe's officially retired, but he remains the main advisor for Sir Humphrey and the power dynamic between them remains unchanged, which means he retains a good deal of his old influence. Chronic Backstabbing Disorder: When Sir Humphrey is revealed to have failed to spot that a former senior civil servant was secretly a KGB agent, he angrily protests that Sir Arnold told him to find said civil servant innocent, but Sir Arnold sternly denies having any recollection of this. He correctly points out that neither he nor Humphrey has any material evidence of the instructions that the latter was given, but it's clear that he has no intention ofTaking the Bulletfor Humphrey's sake. Four Eyes, Zero Soul: Not out-and-outevil, mind (well... probably), but he is a stone-cold bureaucrat who wields power and influence Sir Humphrey could only dream of, and has glasses. The Man Is Sticking It to the Man: Goes from the most powerful civil servant in the government to being the head administrator of the Campaign for Freedom of Information, which nominally is about releasing information that Arnold and his ilk have always tried to suppress. Sure enough, he'll sometimes use his influence to reveal or suppress information just to help Humphrey or the Civil Service.

characters.name: Sir Frank
characters.description: The Alleged Expert: When the government is facing an economic crisis, Hacker complains that Sir Humphrey and Sir Frank didn't warn him about it. Bernard replies that Sir Humphrey doesn't know much about economics because he read Classics at Oxford, and Sir Frank is at an even greater disadvantage in understanding economics- he's an economist. Bystander Syndrome: When told about a massive scandal in the London banking system which Humphrey needs covered up in order to ensure the government's stability, Gordon says he doesn't care because he won't personally be affected if the scandal is revealed. It's only when Humphrey explains that Hacker will blame the Chancellor (head of the Treasury) for the scandal and the Chancellor will blame Gordon in turn that Gordon decides to help with the cover-up. Sesquipedalian Loquaciousness: Not quite as effective with it as Sir Humphrey, but he can still do it enough that Jim figures he could do at least part of his job. Smug Snake: He often acts this way when he thinks he has this advantage. This is especially true in 'The Key,' when Hacker moots giving Gordon some of Humphrey's authority, and 'A Real Partnership,' where he aims to ram a massive pay increase for the Civil Service through parliament to ensure his colleagues' loyalty. Unfortunately for him, he's not as good as he thinks he is and his plans are scuppered by Hacker and Wainwright (with a little help from Humphrey, who then manages to get the pay increase through on his own). The Starscream: As Permanent Secretary of the most powerful department in the government, he is essentially theNumber Twoof the Civil Service, second only to the Cabinet Secretary. He maintains that he and Humphrey are in fact effectivelyjointheads of the Service, and would love to whittle that power-sharing down to himself.

characters.name: Sir Frederick
characters.description: Large and in Charge: He's the Permanent Secretary of the Foreign and Commonwealth Office, one of the most presigious posts. He's also very rotund, which ends up causing problems during a hastily-convened meeting in Hacker's room on anInterCitySleeper (which, for perspective, have rooms barely any larger than the average home's bathroom). Manipulative Bastard: He does his share of string-pulling. Even Sir Humphrey is hard-pressed to keep up. Spotting the Thread: In "The Official Visit", he's the one who works out that if President Mohammed really did intend to deliver an inflammatory speech encouraging Scotland and Northern Ireland to secede from the United Kingdom, heprobablywouldn't have sent the UK government a copy of it beforehand, and that he likely has some other motive. Suspiciously Similar Substitute: Because John Nettleton wasn't available for the rest of the first season after the pilot episode, Jumbo took over the role originally intended for Sir Arnold to take. Season 2 saw Sir Arnold return as a recurring cast member, ironically this time because it was Savident who was unavailable. What Happened to the Mouse?: He never appears again after the first series and is ultimately replaced by Sir Richard

characters.name: Sir Ian
characters.description: Continuity Snarl: In his first and third appearances he's the Permanent Secretary to the Department of Health and Social Security, which makes him the relevant civil servant for dealing with those episodes' primary issues (a hospital which is staffed full of bureaucrats but isn't treating anyone and a government plan to ban smoking, respectively). However, his second appearance has him as the Permanent Secretary to the Environment. Hypocrite: His most notable scene is him munching fancy smoked salmon sandwiches while talking about how ordinary people are barbarians who care more for hedonistic indulgences than upholding British institutions.

characters.name: Sir Richard
characters.description: Bystander Syndrome: He's aware that Saint George's Island is going to be overthrown in a communist uprising backed by East Yemen, and that this would be a disaster for the citizens there, but he doesn't care because the communists have promised to support Britain in various ways. Smug Snake: Like much of the Civil Service, he's blithely confident that he knows best and that he and his colleagues can ensure the ministers support their proposals rather than the other way around. Strongly Worded Letter: He is deliberately ineffectual when a British citizen is arrested and sentenced to decades in prison in a Middle Eastern country for fear of rocking the boat and compromising British interests in the region. Under his direction the Foreign Office refuses to apply any actual diplomatic pressure beyond a statement of protest, which they even send to the country in question ahead of time for approval.

characters.name: Sir Guy
characters.description: Upper-Class Twit: He cares more about his own luxuries and keeping his family happy than about defense. In one of his major roles he objects to a government plan to relocate military bases into northern England, even though the plan would be good both strategically (since many of the existing bases were in nonsensical locations) and economically (since the plan would create many civilian jobs up north and would pay for itself as the government sold expensive land in the south that was no longer needed for bases). His objection? That he and his family would be too far from 'civilization generally,' by which he meant gentlemen's clubs, sports venues (specifically the aristocratic Wimbledon (Tennis), Henley (Boating), and Ascot (Horse Racing)), and Harrod's (a luxury department store patronized by the wives of senior officers such as himself).

characters.name: Sir Desmond
characters.description: Cliché Storm: In-universe. Tends to speak in these whenever he's asked any difficult questions, to the point where even Hacker, someone who also uses this tactic, calls him out on it. The Ditz: Freely admits that not only does he not understand the workings of the banking industry, but he can't even comprehend most of what's written inThe Financial Times, and only buys it because it's "part of the uniform". Freely admits to Humphrey in his first appearance he doesn't know anything, despite which he expects to be appointed to a Quango. Flanderization: He seems like a fairly sensible financier—if a bit baffled by Sir Humphrey's antics during a lunch meeting—in his first appearance. The next time he appears he's become aCloudcuckoolander. Of course, due to his age he may have gone senile. Morally Bankrupt Banker: Zigzags it. He's aware of the widespread corruption and bribery part of the banking system, but operates under the honour code of ignoring it unless it becomes a serious problem, and opposes an attempt to install a governor who might be honest because his scrupulous honesty would potentially cause severe economic disaster for Britain. However, he is not out-and-out corrupt himself; as Dorothy puts it, it's not because of any moral backbone. He's just toostupidto be corrupt. The Pirates Who Don't Do Anything: He's never shown doing anything financial, and in fact he doesn't seem to understand how to do such work. He's generally shown scheming with Humphrey for government favors, or having fancy meals while doing the same.

characters.name: The Master and the Bursar
characters.description: Brutal Honesty: The Bursar is very blunt when explaining why the Dean hates Humphrey. Manipulative Bastard: They're adroit at this, maneuvering Hacker into exempting their college from a subsidy cut. They also figure out that the only way to get rid of the Dean is if Hacker gets him a bishopric. Named In The Adaptation: They aren't named in either of their two appearances on the show, but are named in the novelizations. The Pirates Who Don't Do Anything: Just as we never see any of the government characters actually in the Lords or the Commons voting on anything, we never see the Master or the Bursar of Baillie College actually doing anything academic. The two college characters always shown scheming or luxuriating with Humphrey as they lobby him and Hacker for favors and support.

characters.name: Eileen
characters.description: Spear Carrier: She has a few appearances but mostly just shows up to usher people into Humphrey's office.

characters.name: Prime Minister
characters.description: Bad Boss: It's stated in the series andespecially the booksthat he and Hacker had a somewhat adversarial relationship (Hacker having run a rival contender's in-party campaign for nomination as Prime Minister – and while they lost to the PM, he still holds a grudge). Indeed, it's stated that the reason Hacker was appointed to the Department of Administrative Affairs when he'd been the Shadow Agriculture Minister for seven years was because the DAA was consideredan unglamorous political graveyard. (That, and the Permanent Secretary at the Ministry of Agriculture had begged not to have him – he'd have known too much!) Indeed early in the series the PM comes close to abolishing the entire department – and by extension Hacker's career – altogether. There is a turning point in their relationship where the PM becomesslightlymore of aBenevolent Boss, but it doesn't last. Dropped a Bridge on Him: He suddenly dies from a heart attack before he can finish his memoirs — and the timing works out quite well for Hacker, as it's implied that the final chapter of said memoirs would have blown the whistle on Hacker's ascending to power by engineering the "Eurosausage" scandal. The Friend No One Likes: Apparently no more popular in political circles than he was in his own party. Pretty much everyone shows up to his funeral not to pay respects but to have a quiet opportunity to talk business. When Annie asks if anyone's going to actuallymournhim, Jim says if the only people who showed up were there to do that they wouldn't have enough people to fill the vestibule. The Ghost: Never actually appears on-screen at any point, nor is his voice heard. Heck,his name is never stated onscreen and only appears in the novelization! Invisible President: We never see him until Hacker himself gets the job.

characters.name: Vic
characters.description: Demoted to Extra: He was originally intended to be a regular character, as evidenced by Jewesbury being listed among the main cast in the pilot episode's title sequence. However, the creators couldn't think of anything for Gould to do in future episodes other than just yell at Hacker for making mistakes and/or not managing to get the government's policies past Sir Humphrey, meaning he only made one more appearance, in the third-to-lastYes Ministerepisode. The Dreaded: Hacker visibly fears him, and describes him to Annie as a "terrorist". Sir Humphrey and Bernard exploit this to prevent Hacker from blowing the whistle on an arms-trading deal that would severely embarrass and perhaps bring down the government. No Celebrities Were Harmed: May be one to Michael Cocks, the Chief Whip of the Labour Party at the time the series was written. Put on a Bus: He no longer occupies the Chief Whip's position in the final episode of "Yes Minister", and is replaced by an MP called Jeffrey,alternately playedby James Grout and Peter Cartwright.

characters.name: Sir Mark
characters.description: The Chessmaster: The Prime Minister wants an Integrated Transport Policy, but while that policy will boost the government's overall popularity, it will require severe tradeoffs and thus likely destroy the career of whoever actually implements it. He and Sir Arnold thus can't find anyone who will agree to be in charge of the policy. The solution Mark accepts is to isolate Hacker from Humphrey and then manipulate him into accepting the title of 'Transport Supremo' before his own officials can brief him about why he shouldn't do that. His ploy is initially successful and Hacker takes the job, though he and Humphrey ultimately manage to wriggle out of it. Enemy Mine: Despite usually being at loggerheads with the Civil Service, he teams up with Sir Arnold to lumber Hacker and the DAA with the Integrated Transport Policy as they agree that he is the ideal candidate from both Number 10 and the Service's viewpoint: someone who will produce "lots of activity but no actual achievement". Punny Name: His name is a reference to the British supermarket chain Marks & Spencer.

characters.name: Kumranistan Ambassador
characters.description: Culture Justifies Everything: A horrifying instance; the ambassador argues that Hacker and his civil servants should excuse Aitikeev's pedophilia on the grounds that pederasty is common in Kumranistan. Political Overcorrectness: The ambassador argues that Hacker and the others shouldn't be offended by Aitikieev's monstrous request but should instead accept it as just part of Kumranistan's culture.

characters.name: Sir David
characters.description: Meaningful Name: 'Knell' is usually used in the context of 'death knell,' which he certainly is with respect to Hacker's remaining prestige and influence.
